docs.title: odoopy
docs.text:
Async Python SDK for Odoo JSON-RPC

odoopy gives you a fully typed, async-first client for Odoo's JSON-RPC API. Built on httpx, it works with Odoo 17, 18, and 19.

docs.title: Highlights
docs.text:
• Async from the ground up -- built on httpx.AsyncClient, all calls are non-blocking
  • Full CRUD helpers -- search, read, search_read, create, write, unlink
  • 8 high-level services -- mail, modules, attendance, timesheets, accounting, URLs, properties, CDC
  • Safety guards -- optional confirmation callback before write/delete operations
  • Structured errors -- typed exception hierarchy with to_json() serialisation
  • Testcontainers integration -- spin up real Odoo instances in Docker for integration tests
  • Supports Odoo 17, 18, and 19

docs.title: Quick install
docs.text:
uv add odoopy-client

Or with pip:

pip install odoopy-client

docs.title: Hello, Odoo
docs.text:
import asyncio
from odoopy_client import create_client

async def main():
    client = await create_client()  # reads ODOO_* env vars
    partners = await client.search_read(
        "res.partner",
        [("is_company", "=", True)],
        fields=["name", "email"],
        limit=5,
    )
    for p in partners:
        print(p["name"], p["email"])
    await client.aclose()

asyncio.run(main())

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
• Getting Started -- install, configure, first CRUD operations
  • Services -- high-level APIs for mail, attendance, accounting, and more
  • Guides -- patterns for multi-environment, safety, error handling
  • API Reference -- full autodoc of every class and function

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
uvpip 
uv add odoopy-client

pip install odoopy-client

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
odoopy reads connection details from environment variables. Set these before running your code:

Variable Alias Description ODOO_URL Base URL of the Odoo instance (e.g. https://mycompany.odoo.com) ODOO_DB ODOO_DATABASE Database name ODOO_USER ODOO_USERNAME Login username ODOO_PASSWORD Login password 
export ODOO_URL=https://mycompany.odoo.com
export ODOO_DB=mycompany
export ODOO_USER=admin
export ODOO_PASSWORD=secret

docs.title: Creating a client
docs.text:
The fastest way to get started is create_client, which reads the environment and authenticates in one call:

from odoopy_client import create_client

client = await create_client()

For more control, build the config yourself:

from odoopy_client import OdooClient, OdooClientConfig

config = OdooClientConfig(
    url="https://mycompany.odoo.com",
    database="mycompany",
    username="admin",
    password="secret",
)
client = OdooClient(config)
await client.authenticate()

docs.title: search_read
docs.text:
Fetch records matching a domain with specific fields:

partners = await client.search_read(
    "res.partner",
    [("is_company", "=", True)],
    fields=["name", "email", "phone"],
    limit=10,
    order="name asc",
)

docs.title: search
docs.text:
Return only IDs:

ids = await client.search("res.partner", [("country_id.code", "=", "ES")])

docs.title: read
docs.text:
Read specific records by ID:

records = await client.read("res.partner", [1, 2, 3], fields=["name", "email"])

docs.title: search_count
docs.text:
Count matching records without fetching them:

total = await client.search_count("sale.order", [("state", "=", "sale")])

docs.title: create
docs.text:
Create a new record and get its ID back:

partner_id = await client.create("res.partner", {
    "name": "Acme Corp",
    "email": "info@acme.com",
    "is_company": True,
})

docs.title: write
docs.text:
Update one or more records:

await client.write("res.partner", partner_id, {"phone": "+34 600 000 000"})

docs.title: unlink
docs.text:
Delete records:

await client.unlink("res.partner", [partner_id])

docs.title: Using services
docs.text:
High-level services are available as cached properties on the client:

# Post an internal note
await client.mail.post_internal_note("sale.order", 42, "Reviewed and approved")

# Check if a module is installed
installed = await client.modules.is_module_installed("sale")

# Get a shareable URL for a record
url = await client.urls.get_record_url("sale.order", 42)

See the Services section for full documentation of each service.

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
Always close the client when you are done:

await client.aclose()

docs.title: Testing with odoopy-testcontainers
docs.text: odoopy-testcontainers lets you spin up real Odoo + PostgreSQL containers for integration tests. No mocks, no stubs -- your tests run against a live Odoo instance.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
uv add --dev odoopy-testcontainers

Requires Docker to be running on your machine or CI environment.

docs.title: Pytest fixture
docs.text:
import pytest
from odoopy_testcontainers import OdooTestContainer

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
async def odoo():
    """Start an Odoo container with the sale module installed."""
    container = OdooTestContainer(modules=["sale"])
    started = await container.start()
    yield started
    await started.cleanup()

@pytest.fixture
def client(odoo):
    """Pre-authenticated OdooClient."""
    return odoo.client

Use the fixture in your tests:

async def test_create_partner(client):
    partner_id = await client.create("res.partner", {
        "name": "Test Partner",
        "email": "test@example.com",
    })
    assert partner_id > 0

    records = await client.read("res.partner", partner_id, fields=["name"])
    assert records[0]["name"] == "Test Partner"

docs.title: OdooTestContainer options
docs.text: Parameter Type Default Description modules list[str] [] Odoo modules to install after startup database str "test_odoo" Database name admin_password str "admin" Admin password startup_timeout int 300 Max seconds to wait for Odoo to be ready env dict[str, str] {} Extra environment variables for the Odoo container

docs.title: Seed-aware startup
docs.text:
If you have a pre-seeded PostgreSQL image (with modules already installed), the container will detect it via the ODOO_SEED_IMAGE environment variable and skip the slow --init base step. Only modules not already present in the seed are installed at startup.

This dramatically reduces test startup time -- from minutes to seconds.

# Use a pre-seeded image
export ODOO_SEED_IMAGE=registry.example.com/odoo-seed:18

docs.title: What you get back
docs.text:
container.start() returns a StartedOdooContainer with:

Attribute Type Description client OdooClient Pre-authenticated client module_manager ModuleManager Module manager for runtime installs url str Odoo base URL (localhost + mapped port) database str Database name

docs.title: CI matrix for multiple Odoo versions
docs.text:
Test against Odoo 17, 18, and 19 in parallel by setting ODOO_VERSION:

# .github/workflows/test.yml
jobs:
  test:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        odoo-version: ["17.0", "18.0", "19.0"]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v5
      - run: uv sync
      - run: uv run pytest -m integration
        env:
          ODOO_VERSION: ${{ matrix.odoo-version }}

docs.title: Environment variables
docs.text: Variable Description ODOO_VERSION Odoo Docker image tag (e.g. 18.0, 17.0). Defaults to latest. ODOO_SEED_IMAGE Pre-seeded PostgreSQL image for faster startup

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Use scope="session" on the fixture to avoid restarting containers per test
  • The module_manager on the started container can install additional modules at runtime if a specific test needs them
  • Container startup waits for a successful /web/session/authenticate call, not just log output, so your client is guaranteed ready

docs.title: odoopy_client.OdooClient
docs.text: Async Odoo client wrapping JsonRpcTransport with safety checks.

docs.title: odoopy_client.config_from_env(prefix='ODOO')
docs.text:
Build OdooClientConfig from environment variables.

Reads

{prefix}_URL {prefix}_DB (alias: {prefix}_DATABASE) {prefix}_USER (alias: {prefix}_USERNAME) {prefix}_PASSWORD

docs.title: odoopy_client.create_client(prefix='ODOO') async
docs.text: Create and authenticate an OdooClient from environment variables.

docs.title: odoopy_client.OdooError
docs.text:
Bases: Exception

Base class for all Odoo client errors.

docs.title: odoopy_client.OdooRpcError
docs.text:
Bases: OdooError

Generic RPC error returned by the Odoo server.

docs.title: odoopy_client.OdooAuthError
docs.text:
Bases: OdooRpcError

Authentication / AccessDenied error.

docs.title: odoopy_client.OdooNetworkError
docs.text:
Bases: OdooRpcError

Network / connection error.

docs.title: odoopy_client.OdooTimeoutError
docs.text:
Bases: OdooNetworkError

Request timeout.

docs.title: odoopy_client.OdooValidationError
docs.text:
Bases: OdooRpcError

ValidationError or UserError from Odoo.

docs.title: odoopy_client.OdooAccessError
docs.text:
Bases: OdooRpcError

ACL violation.

docs.title: odoopy_client.OdooMissingError
docs.text:
Bases: OdooRpcError

Record not found (MissingError).

docs.title: odoopy_client.OdooSafetyError
docs.text:
Bases: OdooError

Local safety guard blocked the operation — NOT an RPC error.

docs.title: odoopy_client.safety.resolve_safety_context(client_context=None, *, undefined=False)
docs.text:
Resolve the effective safety context.

  • undefined=True: the caller has no opinion — fall back to global default.
  • undefined=False (default): client_context is the caller's explicit choice (even None meaning "disable safety").

docs.title: odoopy_client.MailService
docs.text: High-level mail / messaging service for Odoo.

docs.title: odoopy_client.ModuleManager
docs.text: High-level service for managing Odoo modules.

docs.title: install_module(module_name) async
docs.text:
Install a module by name.

Returns updated module info dict. Raises RuntimeError if module not found.

docs.title: uninstall_module(module_name) async
docs.text:
Uninstall a module by name.

Returns updated module info dict. Raises RuntimeError if module not found.

docs.title: upgrade_module(module_name) async
docs.text:
Upgrade a module by name. Module must be installed.

Returns updated module info dict. Raises RuntimeError if module not found or not installed.

docs.title: list_modules(*, state=None, application=None, limit=None, offset=None) async
docs.text: List modules with optional filters.

docs.title: get_module_info(module_name) async
docs.text:
Return detailed info for a single module.

Raises RuntimeError if module not found.

docs.title: is_module_installed(module_name) async
docs.text: Return True if the named module is installed.

docs.title: odoopy_client.AttendanceService
docs.text: High-level attendance service for Odoo HR.

docs.title: odoopy_client.TimesheetsService
docs.text: High-level timesheet service for Odoo.

docs.title: odoopy_client.AccountingService
docs.text: High-level accounting service for Odoo.

docs.title: odoopy_client.UrlService
docs.text: High-level URL builder service for Odoo.

docs.title: odoopy_client.PropertiesService
docs.text: High-level service for Odoo properties fields.

docs.title: odoopy_client.CdcService
docs.text: High-level Change Data Capture service for Odoo.

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: odoopy provides a structured error hierarchy so you can catch specific failure types and handle them appropriately.

docs.title: Error hierarchy
docs.text:
OdooError
├── OdooRpcError (code, data)
│   ├── OdooAuthError
│   ├── OdooNetworkError
│   │   └── OdooTimeoutError
│   ├── OdooValidationError
│   ├── OdooAccessError
│   └── OdooMissingError
└── OdooSafetyError (local, not RPC)

docs.title: RPC errors
docs.text: All RPC errors carry optional code and data fields from the Odoo JSON-RPC response.

docs.title: OdooAuthError
docs.text:
Raised when authentication fails or the session expires:

from odoopy_client import OdooAuthError

try:
    await client.authenticate()
except OdooAuthError:
    print("Bad credentials or session expired")

docs.title: OdooValidationError
docs.text:
Maps to Odoo's ValidationError and UserError. Commonly raised when business rules are violated:

from odoopy_client import OdooValidationError

try:
    await client.write("sale.order", 42, {"state": "invalid"})
except OdooValidationError as e:
    print(f"Validation failed: {e}")

docs.title: OdooAccessError
docs.text:
ACL or record rule violation:

from odoopy_client import OdooAccessError

try:
    await client.read("hr.employee", [1], fields=["name"])
except OdooAccessError:
    print("Insufficient permissions")

docs.title: OdooMissingError
docs.text:
The requested record does not exist:

from odoopy_client import OdooMissingError

try:
    await client.read("res.partner", [999999])
except OdooMissingError:
    print("Record not found")

docs.title: OdooNetworkError / OdooTimeoutError
docs.text:
Connection failures and timeouts:

from odoopy_client import OdooNetworkError, OdooTimeoutError

try:
    partners = await client.search_read("res.partner", [])
except OdooTimeoutError:
    print("Request timed out")
except OdooNetworkError:
    print("Could not reach Odoo server")

docs.title: OdooSafetyError
docs.text:
Raised locally when a safety guard blocks an operation. No network call is made:

from odoopy_client import OdooSafetyError

try:
    await client.unlink("res.partner", [1])
except OdooSafetyError as e:
    print(f"Blocked: {e.operation.description}")

docs.title: Structured output with to_json()
docs.text:
Every error supports to_json() for logging or API responses:

try:
    await client.authenticate()
except OdooAuthError as e:
    print(e.to_json())
    # {"error": "AUTH_ERROR", "message": "Authentication failed", "details": ...}

docs.title: RPC error classification
docs.text:
When Odoo returns an error, odoopy classifies it in two steps:

  1. Check exception_type in the response -- maps directly to ValidationError, AccessError, MissingError, etc.
  2. Fall back to data.name -- older Odoo versions use this field instead

This two-step approach ensures correct classification across Odoo 17-19.

docs.title: Retry on network errors
docs.text:
import asyncio
from odoopy_client import OdooNetworkError

async def resilient_read(client, model, ids, retries=3):
    for attempt in range(retries):
        try:
            return await client.read(model, ids)
        except OdooNetworkError:
            if attempt == retries - 1:
                raise
            await asyncio.sleep(2 ** attempt)

docs.title: Catch-all with specifics
docs.text:
from odoopy_client import OdooError, OdooAuthError, OdooValidationError

try:
    await client.create("sale.order", values)
except OdooAuthError:
    await client.authenticate()  # re-auth and retry
except OdooValidationError as e:
    log.warning("Business rule violated: %s", e)
except OdooError as e:
    log.error("Unexpected Odoo error: %s", e.to_json())

docs.title: Multi-Environment Configuration
docs.text: odoopy supports connecting to multiple Odoo instances from the same application using environment variable prefixes.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text: config_from_env and create_client accept a prefix argument (default: "ODOO"). The prefix is prepended to _URL, _DB, _USER, and _PASSWORD.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Set environment variables for each environment:

# Production
export ODOO_PROD_URL=https://prod.mycompany.com
export ODOO_PROD_DB=production
export ODOO_PROD_USER=api_user
export ODOO_PROD_PASSWORD=prod_secret

# Staging
export ODOO_STG_URL=https://staging.mycompany.com
export ODOO_STG_DB=staging
export ODOO_STG_USER=api_user
export ODOO_STG_PASSWORD=stg_secret

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from odoopy_client import create_client

async def main():
    prod = await create_client("ODOO_PROD")
    staging = await create_client("ODOO_STG")

    # Compare record counts across environments
    prod_count = await prod.search_count("sale.order", [("state", "=", "sale")])
    stg_count = await staging.search_count("sale.order", [("state", "=", "sale")])
    print(f"Production: {prod_count} confirmed orders")
    print(f"Staging:    {stg_count} confirmed orders")

    await prod.aclose()
    await staging.aclose()

docs.title: Manual config
docs.text:
For environments where variables do not follow the prefix convention, build configs directly:

from odoopy_client import OdooClient, OdooClientConfig

prod_config = OdooClientConfig(
    url="https://prod.mycompany.com",
    database="production",
    username="api_user",
    password="prod_secret",
)
prod = OdooClient(prod_config)
await prod.authenticate()

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Use read-only credentials for production when possible
  • Pair multi-environment setups with safety guards to prevent accidental writes to production
  • Each client maintains its own HTTP session and authentication state

docs.title: Safety Guards
docs.text: odoopy includes an optional safety system that intercepts write and delete operations before they reach the Odoo server. This is useful for CLI tools, scripts, and multi-environment setups where accidental mutations must be prevented.

docs.title: Off by default
docs.text: Safety guards are disabled by default for backwards compatibility. You opt in by providing a SafetyContext.

docs.title: SafetyContext
docs.text:
A SafetyContext holds a single async confirm callback. Before any non-read operation, odoopy calls this function with an OperationInfo describing what is about to happen. If the callback returns False, the operation is blocked locally and OdooSafetyError is raised.

from odoopy_client import SafetyContext, OperationInfo

async def confirm_handler(op: OperationInfo) -> bool:
    print(f"About to {op.level} on {op.model}: {op.description}")
    answer = input("Allow? [y/N] ")
    return answer.lower() == "y"

safety = SafetyContext(confirm=confirm_handler)

docs.title: Safety levels
docs.text:
Level When Blocked? READ search, read, search_read, search_count, etc. Never WRITE create, write, message_post, and any unknown method If confirm returns False DELETE unlink If confirm returns False

The level is inferred from the RPC method name via infer_safety_level.

docs.title: Per-client safety
docs.text:
Pass the context when creating the config:

from odoopy_client import OdooClient, OdooClientConfig

config = OdooClientConfig(
    url="https://prod.mycompany.com",
    database="production",
    username="admin",
    password="secret",
    safety=safety,
)
client = OdooClient(config)

Or set it after creation:

client.set_safety_context(safety)

docs.title: Global default
docs.text:
Set a default for all clients that do not have an explicit safety context:

from odoopy_client.safety import set_default_safety_context

set_default_safety_context(safety)

Clients with an explicit context (even None for "disabled") ignore the global default.

docs.title: OdooSafetyError
docs.text:
When an operation is blocked, OdooSafetyError is raised. This is a local error -- no RPC call is made. It includes the OperationInfo that was rejected:

from odoopy_client import OdooSafetyError

try:
    await client.unlink("res.partner", [1])
except OdooSafetyError as e:
    print(e.operation.model)  # "res.partner"
    print(e.operation.level)  # "DELETE"

docs.title: Auto-approve reads pattern
docs.text:
A common pattern is to auto-approve reads and prompt only for writes/deletes:

async def interactive_confirm(op: OperationInfo) -> bool:
    if op.level == "READ":
        return True  # never reached, but defensive
    return input(f"Allow {op.level} on {op.model}? [y/N] ").lower() == "y"

Note that READ operations are never sent to the confirm callback -- they are short-circuited internally.

docs.title: Accounting Service
docs.text: Access via client.accounting. Provides utilities for working with account.move, account.move.line, and journal entries.

docs.title: discover_cash_accounts
docs.text:
Finds cash and bank accounts by looking at journals with type bank or cash, not by account code prefix. This is more reliable across localisations:

accounts = await client.accounting.discover_cash_accounts()
for a in accounts:
    print(a.name, a.code)

Returns a list of CashAccount dataclasses with id, name, code, and company_id.

docs.title: get_cash_account_ids
docs.text:
Convenience wrapper that returns just the account IDs:

ids = await client.accounting.get_cash_account_ids()

docs.title: trace_reconciliation
docs.text:
Follow the full_reconcile_id link across move lines to trace how a payment was reconciled with invoices:

trace = await client.accounting.trace_reconciliation(move_line_id=1042)
print(f"Reconcile group: {trace.full_reconcile_id}")
for line in trace.lines:
    print(f"  Move {line.move_id}: debit={line.debit} credit={line.credit}")

docs.title: resolve_partner_from_move
docs.text:
Find the partner on a bank statement entry. The resolution order is:

  1. Check the cash/bank line itself for a partner_id
  2. Check counterpart lines in the reconciliation
  3. Detect batch payments and resolve from there
partner = await client.accounting.resolve_partner_from_move(move_id=500)
if partner:
    print(partner.name, partner.vat)

docs.title: is_closing_entry
docs.text:
Detect year-end closing entries by checking for accounts in the 129x range:

if await client.accounting.is_closing_entry(move_id=999):
    print("This is a closing entry, skip it")

docs.title: calculate_days_to_pay
docs.text:
Calculate the number of days between invoice date and payment date by tracing the reconciliation chain:

result = await client.accounting.calculate_days_to_pay(move_id=450)
if result.days_to_pay is not None:
    print(f"Paid in {result.days_to_pay} days")
else:
    print("Not yet paid")

Returns a DaysToPayResult with move_id, invoice_date, payment_date, and days_to_pay.

docs.title: get_cash_balance
docs.text:
Sum posted balances for a journal up to the current date:

balance = await client.accounting.get_cash_balance(journal_id=7)
print(f"Cash balance: {balance:.2f}")

docs.title: get_posted_move_lines
docs.text:
Convenience wrapper that adds parent_state=posted to any domain you provide:

lines = await client.accounting.get_posted_move_lines(
    [("account_id", "=", 42)],
    limit=100,
    order="date desc",
)

docs.title: Attendance Service
docs.text: Access via client.attendance. Manages clock-in/clock-out operations against Odoo's hr.attendance model.

docs.title: Employee resolution
docs.text: All methods accept an optional employee_id. When omitted, the service resolves the employee linked to the authenticated user via hr.employee. This means the most common case (clocking yourself in) requires no extra arguments.

docs.title: clock_in
docs.text:
record = await client.attendance.clock_in()
print(record.check_in)  # "2026-03-24 08:30:00"

With an explicit employee:

record = await client.attendance.clock_in(employee_id=7)

docs.title: clock_out
docs.text:
record = await client.attendance.clock_out()
print(record.check_out)  # "2026-03-24 17:00:00"

docs.title: get_status
docs.text:
Check whether an employee is currently clocked in:

status = await client.attendance.get_status()
if status.is_clocked_in:
    print(f"Clocked in since {status.last_check_in}")

docs.title: list_attendances
docs.text:
List attendance records with filtering options:

from odoopy_client.services.attendance.types import AttendanceListOptions

records = await client.attendance.list_attendances(
    AttendanceListOptions(limit=10, order="check_in desc")
)
for r in records:
    print(r.check_in, r.check_out)

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Clock in twice -- raises an error if the employee already has an open attendance (no check_out).
  • Clock out without being clocked in -- raises an error if no open attendance exists.

docs.title: Full example
docs.text:
from odoopy_client import create_client

client = await create_client()

# Clock in
await client.attendance.clock_in()

# Check status
status = await client.attendance.get_status()
assert status.is_clocked_in

# Clock out
record = await client.attendance.clock_out()
print(f"Worked from {record.check_in} to {record.check_out}")

await client.aclose()

docs.title: CDC Service (Change Data Capture)
docs.text: Access via client.cdc. Reads Odoo's native field-change audit log stored in mail.tracking.value.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• The model must inherit mail.thread
  • Fields you want to track must have tracking=True in their definition

docs.title: check
docs.text:
Diagnostic method -- verify whether a model supports change tracking:

result = await client.cdc.check("sale.order")
print(result.has_tracking)     # True
print(result.tracked_fields)   # ["state", "partner_id", "amount_total", ...]

Returns a CdcCheckResult with model, has_tracking, and tracked_fields.

docs.title: get_history
docs.text:
Fetch all tracked changes for a single record:

from odoopy_client.services.cdc.types import GetHistoryOptions

events = await client.cdc.get_history(
    "sale.order",
    42,
    GetHistoryOptions(field_names=["state"], limit=10),
)
for event in events:
    print(f"{event.date}: {event.field_description} "
          f"changed from {event.old_value.display!r} "
          f"to {event.new_value.display!r}")

docs.title: get_feed
docs.text:
Async iterator over all changes for a model, paginated automatically:

from odoopy_client.services.cdc.types import GetFeedOptions

feed = await client.cdc.get_feed(
    GetFeedOptions(model="sale.order", batch_size=200)
)
async for event in feed:
    print(event.id, event.field_name, event.new_value.display)

docs.title: Cursor-based pagination
docs.text:
The feed uses id-based cursors (not timestamps). This avoids sub-second precision issues with create_date that could cause events to be skipped or duplicated.

Set since_id to resume from a previous position:

feed = await client.cdc.get_feed(
    GetFeedOptions(model="sale.order", since_id=last_seen_id)
)

docs.title: Field cache
docs.text: Field metadata (ir.model.fields) is cached across pages within a single feed iteration to minimise RPC calls.

docs.title: TrackingEvent
docs.text: Field Type Description id int mail.tracking.value ID field_name str Technical field name field_description str Human-readable field label old_value TypedValue Previous value new_value TypedValue New value date str ISO datetime of the change author str User who made the change message_id int \| None Related mail.message ID

docs.title: TypedValue
docs.text: Field Type Description raw Any Raw value from Odoo display str Human-readable display string

docs.title: GetFeedOptions
docs.text: Field Type Default Description model str required Model technical name res_ids list[int] \| None None Filter to specific record IDs field_names list[str] \| None None Filter to specific fields batch_size int 100 Records per RPC page since_id int 0 Resume cursor

docs.title: Mail Service
docs.text: Access via client.mail. Provides methods for posting messages on Odoo records that inherit mail.thread.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: The target model must inherit mail.thread. Most standard Odoo business models (sale.order, account.move, helpdesk.ticket, etc.) already do.

docs.title: post_internal_note
docs.text:
Post a staff-only internal note. Uses subtype mail.mt_note, so followers are not notified by email.

msg_id = await client.mail.post_internal_note(
    "sale.order",
    42,
    "Customer confirmed delivery address by phone.",
)

docs.title: post_open_message
docs.text:
Post a public message that triggers follower notifications:

from odoopy_client.services.mail.types import PostMessageOptions

msg_id = await client.mail.post_open_message(
    "sale.order",
    42,
    "Your order has been shipped!",
    options=PostMessageOptions(partner_ids=[15]),
)

docs.title: HTML body handling
docs.text:
• Plain text is automatically wrapped in <p> tags before sending.
  • HTML strings (already containing tags) are sent as-is.
  • An empty body raises OdooValidationError.

docs.title: PostMessageOptions
docs.text: Field Type Description partner_ids list[int] Additional partners to notify (beyond followers) attachment_ids list[int] IDs of ir.attachment records to attach

docs.title: Odoo 19 compatibility
docs.text: Odoo 19 changed message_post to return list[int] instead of int. odoopy handles this automatically -- both post_internal_note and post_open_message always return a single int message ID regardless of Odoo version.

docs.title: Modules Service
docs.text: Access via client.modules. Manages Odoo module installation, upgrades, and queries against ir.module.module.

docs.title: install_module
docs.text:
Install a module by technical name. If already installed, the call is a no-op:

info = await client.modules.install_module("sale")
print(info["state"])  # "installed"

docs.title: uninstall_module
docs.text: info = await client.modules.uninstall_module("sale")

docs.title: upgrade_module
docs.text:
Upgrade an already-installed module. Raises RuntimeError if the module is not currently installed:

info = await client.modules.upgrade_module("sale")

docs.title: is_module_installed
docs.text:
Quick boolean check:

if await client.modules.is_module_installed("hr_timesheet"):
    print("Timesheets module is active")

docs.title: get_module_info
docs.text:
Fetch detailed info for a single module:

info = await client.modules.get_module_info("sale")
print(info["shortdesc"], info["installed_version"])

docs.title: list_modules
docs.text:
List modules with optional filters:

# All installed applications
apps = await client.modules.list_modules(state="installed", application=True)

# First 20 modules regardless of state
all_mods = await client.modules.list_modules(limit=20)

docs.title: ir_cron retry
docs.text: Module install/uninstall/upgrade triggers Odoo server operations that sometimes collide with scheduled actions (ir.cron). When a lock error mentioning ir_cron is detected, odoopy retries up to 3 times with a 5-second delay between attempts.

docs.title: Module states
docs.text:
Odoo modules flow through these states:

uninstalled → installed → to upgrade → installed
                        → to remove  → uninstalled

The install_module method skips the call entirely if the module is already in the installed state.

docs.title: Properties Service
docs.text: Access via client.properties. Handles Odoo's properties fields safely using a read-merge-write pattern.

docs.title: Why read-merge-write?
docs.text:
Odoo properties use full replacement semantics. When you write a properties field, you must send the complete set of property values. If you only send the property you want to change, all other properties on the record are erased.

The properties service solves this by:

  1. Reading the current properties from the record
  2. Converting them to write format
  3. Merging your updates into the full set
  4. Writing the merged result back

docs.title: update_safely
docs.text:
Update one or more properties on a single record:

await client.properties.update_safely(
    "sale.order",
    42,
    "x_properties",  # the properties field name
    {"priority": "high", "reviewed": True},
)

This reads the current value of x_properties on record 42, merges in priority and reviewed, and writes the full set back.

docs.title: update_safely_batch
docs.text:
Same operation but for multiple records in parallel using asyncio.gather:

await client.properties.update_safely_batch(
    "sale.order",
    [42, 43, 44],
    "x_properties",
    {"priority": "high"},
)

Each record is processed independently -- a failure on one record does not prevent the others from being updated.

docs.title: properties_to_write_format
docs.text:
Static utility that converts the read format (list of property dicts) into the write format (flat key-value dict):

# Read format from Odoo:
props = [
    {"name": "priority", "value": "high", "type": "char"},
    {"name": "reviewed", "value": True, "type": "boolean"},
]

# Convert to write format:
write_vals = PropertiesService.properties_to_write_format(props)
# {"priority": "high", "reviewed": True}

This conversion is done automatically inside update_safely -- you only need this method if you are building custom write logic.

docs.title: Timesheets Service
docs.text: Access via client.timesheets. Tracks time on projects via account.analytic.line.

docs.title: Timer workflow
docs.text:
The timer workflow mirrors Odoo's UI timer -- start a timer with zero hours, then stop it to compute the elapsed duration automatically:

from odoopy_client.services.timesheets.types import TimerStartOptions

# Start a timer on a project
entry = await client.timesheets.start_timer(
    TimerStartOptions(project_id=3, task_id=12, description="Bug fix #451")
)
timesheet_id = entry["id"]

# ... do work ...

# Stop the timer -- hours are computed from elapsed time
result = await client.timesheets.stop_timer(timesheet_id)
print(f"Logged {result['unit_amount']:.2f} hours")

docs.title: Manual logging
docs.text:
For time already completed, use log_time with explicit hours:

from odoopy_client.services.timesheets.types import LogTimeOptions

entry = await client.timesheets.log_time(
    LogTimeOptions(
        project_id=3,
        task_id=12,
        hours=2.5,
        description="Code review",
        date="2026-03-24",
    )
)

docs.title: start_timer
docs.text: Create a timesheet entry with unit_amount=0 and the timer flag set. Returns the created record dict.

docs.title: stop_timer
docs.text: Stop a running timer by timesheet ID. Computes elapsed hours from timer_start and writes unit_amount. Returns the updated record dict.

docs.title: get_running_timers
docs.text:
List all currently running timers for an employee:

timers = await client.timesheets.get_running_timers()
for t in timers:
    print(t["id"], t["name"], t["project_id"])

docs.title: log_time
docs.text: Create a completed timesheet entry with explicit hours. Returns the created record dict.

docs.title: list_timesheets
docs.text:
List timesheet entries with optional filters:

from odoopy_client.services.timesheets.types import TimesheetListOptions

entries = await client.timesheets.list_timesheets(
    TimesheetListOptions(project_id=3, limit=20, order="date desc")
)

docs.title: Employee resolution
docs.text: Like the attendance service, start_timer, get_running_timers, and log_time accept an optional employee_id. When omitted, the employee linked to the authenticated user is resolved automatically.

docs.title: URL Service
docs.text: Access via client.urls. Builds Odoo URLs for backend records and portal pages.

docs.title: get_base_url
docs.text:
Reads web.base.url from ir.config_parameter. The result is cached per client instance:

base = await client.urls.get_base_url()
# "https://mycompany.odoo.com"

Pass force_refresh=True to bypass the cache.

docs.title: get_record_url
docs.text:
Build a version-agnostic backend URL for any record:

url = await client.urls.get_record_url("sale.order", 42)
# "https://mycompany.odoo.com/mail/view?model=sale.order&res_id=42"

Why /mail/view?

The Odoo backend URL format has changed across versions (hash fragment in older versions, /odoo/ prefix in newer ones). The /mail/view controller exists on Odoo 14 through 19 and performs a server-side redirect to the correct format. This makes URLs stable across upgrades.

docs.title: get_portal_url
docs.text:
Build a customer-facing portal URL with an access token:

result = await client.urls.get_portal_url("sale.order", 42)
print(result.url)           # full portal URL with token
print(result.access_token)  # the token string, if generated

Portal URLs let external users (customers, vendors) view specific records without logging in.

docs.title: Types
docs.text: Type Fields PortalUrlOptions access_token: bool (default True) PortalUrlResult url: str, access_token: str \| None
